medications.indication_rationale: Sublingual wafer controls vomiting without needing to swallow; source gave ondansetron 4mg SL stat with good effect.
Antihistamine antiemetic with vestibular-suppressant action for ongoing motion sickness; source prescribed promethazine 25mg every 6–8h PRN (adult). Sedating — advise caution.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 25mg orally every 6–8 hours as needed (adult) (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: oral electrolyte solution by small sips
medications.indication_rationale: Small frequent sips maintain hydration without triggering gastric stretch-vomiting.
medications.dose_route_frequency: one small sip (teaspoon) every ~5 minutes (as per source note)

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Vestibular rest — lie still, keep the head as still as possible, keep the room cool and well-ventilated, avoid strong odours; rehydrate by tiny frequent sips.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces vestibular stimulation and allows fluid absorption without vomiting.

patient_education_points.point: This is severe motion sickness (kinetosis) from the rough boat trip — the acute vomiting should settle with the anti-nausea medicine. Keep your head still and rehydrate with tiny sips every few minutes.
A wobbly 'ground moving' feeling can linger for 12–24 hours as it settles.

Call 000 immediately if you get a sudden severe headache, weakness or numbness on one side, slurred speech, or double vision — these could signal a problem in the brain. And go to ED if you still can't keep any fluids down, stop passing urine, or feel very lightheaded.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution; if vertigo persists beyond 24h without a kinetic cause, reconsider vestibular neuritis / central cause.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any central neurological feature — 000; unable to rehydrate — ED.

must_include_items: Screen central (stroke) red flags and acknowledge telehealth cannot exclude a central cause
Antiemetic + small-sip rehydration with vestibular rest
Dehydration safety-net (can't keep fluids / no urine / lightheaded → ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Ondansetron, promethazine, hyoscine or metoclopramide as antiemetic options
errors_of_omission: No central (stroke) screen / not acknowledging telehealth limits
errors_of_commission: Confidently excluding a central cause remotely
Ignoring dehydration in a patient living alone
Advising large-volume drinking that re-triggers vomiting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires a central-vertigo screen with a stroke safety-net, antiemetic + sip-rehydration management, and a dehydration safety-net.
